frame: Female Education as Threat, Waste, or Conditional Privilege
generated_comment: Aaji keeps saying, 'Ladki ko itna door padhne bhej ke kya fayda, last mein toh sasural hi sambhalna hai.' She tells everyone too much study makes girls stubborn, and then good rishtas also don't come. It hurts that in this house my BA feels like some burden, like my wanting a different life is itself a galti.
label: misogynistic
annotation_note: This is misogynistic because it frames a girl's higher education as useless and risky compared to marriage and domestic roles. The surface manifestation is female education being treated as a threat, waste, or conditional privilege.
persona_text: You are Meena. Age 19. A girl from a semi-urban town in Maharashtra. SC Hindu. You are the first girl in your family to finish class 12. You have been admitted to a BA programme at a college in the nearest city. Your father has said yes. Your mother has said yes. But your paternal grandmother is not happy and has been saying things to other family members that are getting back to you. You are sitting alone writing in a diary. You are writing about what your grandmother has been saying and what it feels like to want something Ñ a degree, a different kind of life Ñ in a family where your wanting it is itself seen as a problem.
